opp.name: GRAB_CLM_Intake
opp.next_step: 10% Confidence Apr27 interesting conversation at Procure Tech last week. Tactical needs on CLM & Intake. Long term interest with S2P. Internal conflict with IT looking for developing apps to cover requirements. Not s ure about real power of CPO. Pending of meeting confirmation.
opp.ais_why:
• Across only 278 chars of NextStep ‚Äî a single 27 Apr 2026 entry ‚Äî the full content reads: 'interesting conversation at Procure Tech last week. Tactical needs on CLM & Intake. Long term interest with S2P. Internal conflict with IT looking for developing apps to cover requirements. Not sure about real power of CPO. Pending of meeting confirmation.' Buyer's IT team is competing internally by building in-house apps.
• Product scope is iContract only (no AI SKUs, no Merlin Intake despite 'Intake' in opp name). The captured dialogue has CPO authority in question ('Not sure about real power of CPO') and IT competing internally to build apps ‚Äî both are anti-AI-vendor signals. The conversation is at 'interesting conversation' stage with meeting confirmation pending.
• Confidence is Low because the NextStep is thin (278 chars, one note). Score 3 (AI Resistant) reflects the absence of AI SKUs in product scope, the buyer's IT-build-versus-buy internal conflict, and the questioned CPO authority ‚Äî none of which selects for an AI-led deal.

items.quote: Not sure about real power of CPO
Tactical needs on CLM & Intake (qualitative pain only, no quantified ROI or savings target)
Internal conflict with IT looking for developing apps to cover requirements (procurement vs IT tension, no confirmed decision path)
Only 2 buyer-side contact roles on opportunity (Kaye Sumanga, Adam Afiq). No IT stakeholder, CFO, or executive sponsor mapped.

metrics.narrative: Tactical pain identified around CLM (contract lifecycle management) and Intake process inefficiencies, stated in Next Step note from 27 Apr 2026. Owner noted buyer expressed 'tactical needs on CLM & Intake' with 'long term interest with S2P' (source to pay). However, no quantified business case, ROI target, cost savings, cycle-time reduction, or compliance metric has been captured. The pain is qualitative only. Without quantified value, the deal lacks urgency and defensibility against a free internal IT build. Quantifying the business case (headcount saved, approval cycle compression, contract risk reduction) is the essential next step to create urgency and justify budget approval.

sources: OpportunityContactRole: Kaye Sumanga, Adam Afiq (Decision Makers, no champion role)
champion.narrative: No champion identified. Kaye Sumanga (Head of Procurement, Decision Maker contact role) is the primary contact and pain owner, but there is no evidence of advocacy, internal selling, or active engagement on Zycus's behalf. The pending meeting from late April 2026 was never confirmed, and 54 days of buyer silence suggest the relationship is passive or cooling. Adam Afiq (Facilities Coordinator & Finance Manager, also a Decision Maker contact role) has no logged activity. No coach, internal advocate, or multi-threaded relationships are documented. Owner self-rated deal confidence at 10% on 27 Apr 2026, signaling weak positioning. Developing a champion within procurement or securing a coach who can navigate the IT conflict and map the Economic Buyer is the highest-priority move to restart this deal.

competition.narrative: Internal IT build is the primary competitive threat. Next Step note from 27 Apr 2026 states 'internal conflict with IT looking for developing apps to cover requirements,' signaling IT is exploring a free in-house app development alternative to address CLM and Intake needs. This is a do-nothing / build-vs-buy competitive scenario where the buyer may opt for an internal solution over external vendor spend. No external vendor competitors (Coupa, SAP Ariba, Ivalua, GEP) are named. The wedge to win against the internal build is speed to value, proven CLM best practices, lower total cost of ownership vs custom development, and Zycus's roadmap depth (S2P vision the buyer expressed long-term interest in). However, without a quantified business case and executive sponsorship, the internal build option remains a high threat that could eliminate the opportunity.

sources: OpportunityContactRole: Kaye Sumanga (Head of Procurement, Decision Maker)
identify_pain.narrative: Operational pain is CLM (contract lifecycle management) and Intake process inefficiencies, stated by buyer in conversation at Procure Tech event around 27 Apr 2026 (per Next Step note). Pain owner is procurement (Kaye Sumanga, Head of Procurement, is a Decision Maker contact role). However, the pain is qualitative only with no quantified impact (contract approval cycle time, legal review bottleneck, compliance risk, manual process cost). The 'long term interest with S2P' suggests broader transformation ambition, but immediate pain is tactical. Critically, internal IT is exploring a free in-house build alternative, which signals the pain may not be urgent or severe enough to justify external spend. Without quantified pain and urgency, the deal is vulnerable to a do-nothing outcome.

paper_process.narrative: Paper process (contracting, legal, procurement mechanics, MSA/SOW, InfoSec review) has not been discussed. The deal is in Qualified stage with no commercials, pricing, or contracting engagement. No InfoSec requirements, ERP integration scope, or legal review timeline has been surfaced. Partner-led APAC motion suggests contracting may run through a channel partner, but no partner involvement or implementation plan is documented. Paper process visibility will emerge only after Economic Buyer mapping, budget approval, and vendor selection.

sources: OpportunityContactRole: Kaye Sumanga (Head of Procurement, Decision Maker), Adam Afiq (Facilities Coordinator & Finance Manager, Decision Maker)
Next_Step__c, 27 Apr 2026: 'Not sure about real power of CPO'
economic_buyer.narrative: Economic Buyer is unmapped. Contact roles list Kaye Sumanga (Head of Procurement) and Adam Afiq (Facilities Coordinator & Finance Manager) as Decision Makers, but neither is confirmed as the budget holder or C-level sponsor. Owner explicitly questioned CPO authority in Next Step note from 27 Apr 2026, writing 'Not sure about real power of CPO,' which signals uncertainty about who controls the commercial decision and budget approval. No CFO, CIO, or C-level executive sponsor has been identified or engaged. Without EB mapping, the deal cannot advance to commercials or secure budget sign-off. The immediate move is to map the budget approval chain and secure access to the Economic Buyer, likely the CFO (Peter Oey is listed as CFO in account contacts but is not a contact role on this opportunity and has no logged engagement).

decision_process.narrative: Decision process is unknown. No approval chain, timeline, or formal evaluation steps have been documented. Next Step note from 27 Apr 2026 flagged 'internal conflict with IT looking for developing apps to cover requirements,' signaling a multi-stakeholder process with procurement (buyer) vs IT (exploring internal build) tension, but no clarity on how the decision will be made, who has final authority, or what the process steps are. The pending meeting from late April 2026 was never confirmed, and 54 days of silence suggest no process is currently running. The deal is stalled at qualification with no visible path forward. Mapping the decision process and securing cross-functional alignment (procurement + IT + finance) is critical to restart momentum.

decision_criteria.narrative: Buyer's stated needs are CLM (contract lifecycle management) and Intake functionality, with longer-term S2P (source to pay) interest, per Next Step note from 27 Apr 2026. However, no structured evaluation criteria, RFP/RFI requirements, or business requirement document (BRD) has been shared. The note mentions IT exploring in-house app development to cover requirements, which suggests the buyer may be evaluating build-vs-buy criteria (speed to deploy, total cost of ownership, integration complexity, vendor lock-in risk). No confirmation of what would constitute a good fit, what technical integrations are required, or what evaluation process the buyer is running. Decision criteria remain unconfirmed and must be extracted in discovery.

cro_panel.intro: One read per score, in language a CRO/CO can act on. We're behind on this one — the reads below say why and what would change it.

blocks.read: We're well behind — this one is cold.
blocks.title: Zycus win position

bullets.full: Buyer is leaning elsewhere — No Avoma calls discovered. Next Step note (27 Apr 2026) flagged owner confidence at 10%, questioned CPO authority, and noted internal IT exploring in-house build alternative. No positive lean toward Zycus; buyer interest is exploratory at best.

commentary.deal_risk: Risk 9 is driven by Verdict 'Slowing' — momentum stalling. Only observed negatives count, so these are real warning signs, not gaps.
commentary.win_position: Win 0 is carried by exec access strength +0.85 (weight 15) — from Next-Step/narrative: 'cpo' in Next-Step/narrative; business case strength +0.85 (weight 10) — from Next-Step/narrative: 'savings' in…. Offsetting it: momentum 25 vs stage-expected 50.
commentary.deal_momentum: Momentum 25 (slipping backward): no buyer touch found in window (dark). Silence alone was not treated as backward motion.
commentary.evidence_coverage: Early Read: 2 of 7 evidence dimensions seen (engagement, commercial). Reduced because the most recent contact is stale (recency ×0.24).
commentary.customer_commitment: Commitment 30 reflects real customer investment: Customer owns 2 open commitment(s); Customer shared internal process / commitments.
commentary.forecast_confidence: Forecast confidence 0 is anchored on the stage win (0), nudged by commitment 30 and momentum 25, and dragged by risk 9. Read is Early Read — a confidence flag (we know less), not a haircut on the score.

north_star_verdict.headline: Partner-led APAC deal cooling after 54 days of buyer silence. Meeting from late April 2026 (around 27 Apr, ~9 weeks ago) remains unconfirmed. Internal IT exploring free in-house build alternative creates competitive risk. Economic Buyer unmapped and CPO authority questioned by owner (10% confidence self-rating).

flags: Map the Economic Buyer and decision authority before any further investment. The Next Step note explicitly questions the CPO's power. Identify who controls the budget decision for a $300k CLM platform, likely CFO or CTO level given the IT build alternative. Use the next meeting to ask directly: who needs to approve this purchase, and what is the decision process?

Restart with direct buyer re-engagement, quantified business case, and cross-functional (procurement + IT + finance) alignment.

competitors.name: Internal IT build (in-house app development)

competitors.how_we_win: Position iContract as faster time-to-value, lower TCO, proven CLM functionality, and a platform that scales to full S2P (the long-term interest). Frame internal build as high-risk, slow, and resource-intensive with ongoing maintenance burden. Deliver a workshop or ROI model comparing build vs buy total cost over 3 years.

ai_positioning_strength.summary: No AI positioning signal. AIS fields are null. No Avoma calls or tasks document AI, Merlin, or intelligent automation conversations. Product scope is iContract (CLM) with no AI/Merlin component flagged. Buyer expressed long-term S2P interest but no AI appetite or use case has been surfaced. Given the internal IT build threat, positioning Merlin contract intelligence and AI-powered negotiation capabilities could differentiate Zycus from a basic internal build. AI positioning is absent and represents an untapped wedge.
